Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after my part-time job, but between work shifts and everything else going on, it rarely worked out as planned. I'd often end up cramming late at night or squeezing in reading during my commute. I kept a notebook where I'd write down what I needed to cover, but actually following through was another story entirely.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what shifts I was working that week. Some weeks I'd be consistent for a few days, then something would come up and throw everything off. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any routine I tried to establish. I wished I could be one of those people who studies at the same time every day, but my life just doesn't work like that right now.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about new content or a discussion post, and that would remind me to check in. Occasionally I'd log in just to see if I'd missed anything important, especially when my anxiety was spiking and I felt like I was falling behind everyone else.
Q4: For maybe two or three weeks mid-semester, I did get into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before heading out. It felt good, like I was on top of things for once. But then I had a really stressful week with extra work shifts and a family situation, and that routine just fell apart. I never quite got it back after that.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and articles and actually engage with them deeply, highlighting and taking notes. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things I was confused about. Those 33 peer interactions I had were genuinely valuable to my understanding.
Q6: I barely watched the video lectures, which I know sounds bad given how much content was there. Videos just don't work for my learning style—I zone out and have to rewatch sections multiple times. I kept telling myself I'd go back to them, but I'd always prioritize reading instead. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because I was already overwhelmed with required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand something I was struggling with, I'd make time for it even when exhausted. Anything that felt like busywork or repetition of stuff I already knew, I'd skip. With limited hours and energy, I had to be strategic.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones I'd force myself through even when my anxiety was through the roof. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but then something urgent would always take priority. It's not that I didn't value them—I just had to triage constantly.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the readings to my phone and study on the bus or during breaks at work. I filled two notebooks with handwritten notes this semester. I also used some YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what I didn't understand from the course materials.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be reviewing my printed notes and working through practice problems, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. My best studying often happened with the LMS completely closed—just me, my notes, and a quiet corner somewhere. The LMS would show me as inactive during some of my most productive study sessions.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading once to download it, then spend hours with it over the next week without logging back in. Same with lecture slides—I'd print them out and annotate them by hand. The initial download was just the starting point; the real engagement happened offline where I could focus without notifications and distractions.
Q12: Around week six or seven, everything got harder. My anxiety was at its peak, I was picking up extra shifts because I needed the money, and I started feeling like I was too far behind to catch up. There were days I couldn't even open the LMS because seeing how much I'd missed made me feel paralyzed. The mindfulness exercises helped somewhat, but the overwhelm was real.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The fatigue from working and studying was brutal—sometimes I'd log in and just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. When I was confused about material, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help because I was embarrassed. My job and financial stress definitely competed with coursework for my mental energy.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open while technically doing other things, or I'd scroll through content while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times because my anxiety was making it impossible to concentrate. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually learning.
Q15: I postponed the exam preparation way too long because the thought of it triggered so much anxiety. I knew it was important—obviously—but facing how much I didn't know felt terrifying. Some discussion posts I'd avoid because I didn't feel confident enough in my understanding to contribute. Avoidance became a coping mechanism even though it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, for better or worse. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, which is probably why my assignment scores were actually decent. But it meant I was always in crisis mode rather than learning steadily. The LMS probably shows these huge spikes in my activity around due dates.
Q17: When I got good feedback on an assignment, it genuinely motivated me to engage more with similar content. But when feedback was critical or I got a bad grade, I'd sometimes avoid that topic entirely afterward. The positive feedback helped with my confidence, which I desperately needed given how anxious I was feeling about the course overall.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming for someone in my situation. There was so much content released at once that I never felt caught up. I would have benefited from more scaffolding or smaller chunks. The flexibility was supposed to be helpful, but without structure, I struggled to impose my own discipline given everything else in my life.
Q19: Having all readings available for download was a lifesaver for my situation and learning style. But the sheer volume of video content that I couldn't engage with well made me feel like I was missing half the course. More text-based alternatives to videos would have helped me enormously. The discussion forums were well-designed and I appreciated those.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's trying really hard but struggling to actually complete things. You might be logging in constantly, watching videos, reading materials, genuinely attempting to learn—but then life gets in the way of finishing assignments. High activity can mean high effort without high output, especially when you're dealing with anxiety or other challenges.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My assignment scores were quite high when I did submit, even though my LMS activity was relatively low. I was doing a lot of deep work offline that didn't register. Someone could be efficiently using downloaded materials and external resources, learning effectively without constant platform engagement.
Q22: A course log can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, submission times. But it completely misses the quality of engagement and all the offline work. It can't tell if someone is staring blankly at a screen or deeply focused. It misses the emotional state behind the clicks, the anxiety that might be preventing someone from even logging in.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting genuine mental effort into learning, not just going through the motions. It's about being curious and wanting to connect ideas, even when it's hard. For me, engagement happened most when I was alone with my notes, really wrestling with concepts—not necessarily when I was clicking around the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the hours on the bus with downloaded readings, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the genuine learning that happened away from the screen. It also wouldn't show that I was dealing with financial stress, work exhaustion, and high anxiety while still pushing forward. My effort was so much more than my data suggests.
